speaker: interviewer
text: Rita Childs, take 6.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. It was 1941. From this one job in the diamond industry, I went and I learned the real diamond business. Because they went on strike. And then in-- in-- I still didn't make very much money because I still was an apprentice. When I was just beginning to get money, I was at these people in New Jersey that my mother had given me the address because they knew somebody, each other-- I don't know. Anyway, I was at their house when we heard that Pearl Harbor had been bombed. It was December 7, 1941. And immediately, of course, that same day, the United States went into the war. I was scared stiff. I knew right away what that meant. I knew right away that the mail would be cut off. Because they didn't take any more mail. They would take people. The war had started, and there was no more mail.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to the Red Cross. And they promised to-- they did it once. They once got me some-- oh, I forgot to tell you that my parents had been moved to Opole in Poland. That was in 1941. Is that a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. They-- my mother wrote me this-- we have to-- they had leave. They were all deported to a place called Opole.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-P-O-L. And it was near Lublin in-- in Poland, in a little ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a city or a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: A ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: A ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they all went. And I was still able to write to them. The first I did-- immediately I sent money. And she wrote me, please don't send money. They steal the money. Send packages.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I spent the entire last of '41 until that time sending food packages. You know, there was a place down in New York where they made up the packages and sent them directly. And I paid for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other thing, I send like $5 a month to my-- another uncle, the one that was an accountant. He had been fired, and he didn't know-- he went to Shanghai. He was beaten up, and he didn't want to stay. So he went to Shanghai by himself-- all by himself. He left his wife behind and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, my mother said, please send some money. We can't send money from here. But they will reimburse me. So I used to send money. And I used to send packages.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, of course, when this day came, December 7, 1941, I was scared to death right away. Because I knew this would be the stop of the mail. And you couldn't send anything anymore. Nobody would take it. The post office wouldn't take it. Nobody would take it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was really desperate. I didn't know what to do. I went to the Red Cross. I understood that the Red Cross sometimes did something. So they promised to do the best they can.

speaker: interviewee
text: And once, they got me-- I think two months, later they got me a note saying-- from my mother saying, we're all right. Even though I had gotten letters from them from Opole, there were still letters. And she said, the situation here is terrible. I don't know how we're going to survive. And I have the letters. And-- but don't worry, we'll survive. And at one point, she said, if you could send the tickets over, maybe they-- but I tried to get the money together. I didn't have any money yet. I was only promised I was going to make money. There was nothing I could do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I found out, I was just scared to death. I knew right away that this was going to be awful. And I don't-- and so we went into 1942 in the beginning of the war. And I started to make some money. And I saved it all, and I was going to buy tickets.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that point, it was already late. I couldn't get ahold of my mother. And she couldn't write to me, and I couldn't write to her. And I'd only gotten that one note from the-- from the Red Cross. And they told me right away, this is the only time we can do this, because from now on, we have to look for lost soldiers. And this is our first priority. And the Jews are no longer our priority. They were very frank about it. They were terrible. And so that was-- I was cut off. And then we hobbled from one year to the other. I was sort of on a lost cause. Except that another woman and I-- we took an apartment. We stopped living in furnished rooms. We took a little apartment in Washington Heights for $37.50, a walk-up, five-flight walk-up. And we got some furniture from other people, and we bought secondhand stuff-- not much, just a bed and a dresser and something like that. But we felt like we were human beings. You know, we had our own kitchen. We had-- didn't have to-- so on and so forth. And I was saving money like crazy. And then one day-- I think it was beginning of 1943-- I read in the that the Nazis were in the process eliminating all of the ghettos that they had established. And they had all the names of the ghettos. And Opole was one of them. And that everybody had been either killed there or sent to death camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well immediately, I went to the highest-- all the Jewish organizations in New York. And they told-- you know, I didn't believe it. And they said, unfortunately, it's true. I said, what can I do? How can I-- you can't get in touch with them. They're probably dead. How they die? How do I-- you can't find out. You can't find out. Maybe after the war you can, but right now, we can't do anything for you. And there were hundreds of people, you know. Nothing we could do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that point I had been dating a young man that was in the Army. I'd met him, I think, at a dance or something like that, a German club dance. And he was German. And he was in the American army. And he had lost his parents, too. He said, you know, we should get married. We have no family. We'll start a family. And I was in such desperation, because that night when I read that paper, I think I walked all over Manhattan all night. I just walked the streets. I didn't know what to do. I was just desperate. And I felt guilty. I hadn't gotten them out. I had went to late, maybe would have done it earlier-- all these, if I worked on this, if I would have done that, if he would have done this, if he-- my uncle would have-- you know, all these iffy questions. I could have done it. I was to blame because it was my fault. I didn't do it early enough. I didn't see. I-- I was-- I was too young to understand what was going on. I should have seen it. Now I would know that that man was just stealing money from me, and he wasn't doing anything. But anyway, so I immediately said, you know, that's a good idea. But he was stationed in Georgia. So I took a train to Georgia. I took a vacation. I took a train to Georgia, which a little-- Macon, Georgia. When I got there, I thought I was back in Nazi Germany. Everything was segregated. Even the soldiers in the American uniform, black soldiers, couldn't get a sandwich or cup of coffee anywhere, couldn't drink out of a fountain. And I was just horrified. I was-- and my future husband didn't seem to mind that. And something happened. I must tell you. Because I almost got arrested. I was in the bus, and I came from New York. And I-- there was-- the bus was crowded. And I went to the back, and I sat next to a black woman. And she immediately got up stood. And I realized that I shouldn't have done that. That's when I realized. And the bus driver stopped the bus in the middle of the street, turned around and said, you must be one of them damn Yankees coming here to make problems. We have people arrested for what you just did.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she ran out of the bus, and I ran out of the bus. And I said to my-- Julius was his name. Julius, I can't-- I can't stand this here. I don't know how you-- because he wanted me to live there. He says, all the-- all the wives come down here. We'll get you a furnished room. I said, what will I do here? I was just starting to make money. Well, you can get job as a waitress. I said no, I'm not getting a job as a waitress, and I'm not living down here. So we had-- on our honeymoon, we had our biggest fight. I said, I'm not living here. I don't care what you say. I can't live here. Besides, I'm just making money. I need the money. Maybe there's something I can do. So, how long were you married to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe a year? Because we were fighting. I went once down more. And-- and-- he didn't like the-- the books I read. He didn't like the newspapers I read. I mean, he was trying-- he was a typical German, trying to tell me what to do. And I had been alone for too long and self-supporting that I wasn't going to have somebody tell me what I was going to do. So we had fights all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So finally I said to him-- and then he wanted to start a family. I said, I'm not moving down here and start a family. I couldn't. And I said, you know, we should get divorced. This is ridiculous. We don't have a marriage. We don't agree on anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- but in those days, you couldn't get divorced because-- just because you decided. So I didn't know what to do. So somebody suggested, tell him everything that he does, he gets paid for, so divorce you. Tell him you just left him or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I suggested that to him. He at first didn't want to do it. I said, I'm not coming back. We haven't got a marriage. So he did it, and I got a divorce. So, were you ever married again?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1945, and I became a citizen then. And I left his name, too. And I left his name, and I haven't heard from him since. And I was in the diamond business until 1950 or '51. I was still there. And this was in the-- and when the war ended, immediately I tried to find out whether I could go there and find my parents. And everybody said, you're crazy. You can't go there. The cities have been bombed. You're going to get killed. They're going to kill you. And that was true. People went there, and they got killed. And you can't-- besides, you couldn't get a passport to go there. You wouldn't-- you wouldn't be able to go. So I just had to wait. And then they had a survivors lists. And I went there every Sunday, and I looked at all-- from all countries to see whether there was anybody I knew. There was never anybody. Then, like a couple of months later, I-- they thought they found my brother. Somebody in Italy had the same name and the same birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother found?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wasn't my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We wrote to each other for about two or three months and realized he wasn't-- no, he had nothing to do with that. But he born the same day. So, that was another disappointment. In the meantime, then, I lived by myself because my-- that woman that I lived with, she came from Bulgaria, and she wanted to go back to see her mother or family. She went back, and I was alone. And then I had my own apartment from then on in. But by that time, I made good money. And then I met my other husband at some rally or other. I don't know-- something. [LAUGHS] And he was a musician. And we met, and we-- things. And we got married on-- in 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have two-- two boys. The first one--

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first one is Paul. He was born in 1953. And he's now 42. [LAUGHS] I can't believe it. And then almost four years later, I had another boy named Jeffrey. And-- and Paul lives here in Agoura, and Jeffrey lives in-- he got a job in-- in a little town near Seattle. He lives-- he works in Seattle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, Jeffrey was the one I got from-- married first. And he had two children, a little girl, whose name is Jennifer, and she's 10 years old now, and a little boy, Nicholas, who's going to be eight in November. And I go as often as I can to visit them. And I have my other son, Paul, who was married and got divorced and married again. And he has two children, a four-- almost four and one and a half. And they're adorable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let me ask you two more questions. Can you finish telling us what-- what came of your parents? Did you ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: I never found out. I-- and-- my-- my-- I did go, finally-- when somebody said, you know, you can get a pension, but you have to prove that you-- I have to prove that my parents are dead. I went back to Vienna. And I think it was in '69. You can get reparations, somebody told me. So I went back to Vienna. And I went to the-- what they call the Israeli [INAUDIBLE], which is Jewish-- it's like a Jewish agency, where everybody's registered that's Jewish, and they knew where everybody was. And I went there, and I explained my situation. They went to a big cabinet, and they pulled out a book. And there was my whole history. My parents' marriage was recorded, my birth was recorded-- everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then took out-- I said, well-- I said, when were they deported? They took out another little thing, and I got a little slip, stating that they were deported on such-and-such a date in 1941 and not heard of again. And they were considered dead. So I-- I gave that to the German people first. And they gave me $5,000. That was it. Then I thought that was it, and I came to California. And then somebody said, you know, you can get a pension from Austria. There are people who get a pension from Austria. So I had to start all over again. And I-- I went to the consulate, and they suggested I get a German lawyer or an Austrian lawyer. And he couldn't find my school where I went to in Vienna. He found my-- found everything, my brother's school, everything but my school. So I was cheated out of a few years there. But I finally-- I'm getting a pension from them. Let me ask you one last question. If you were to look back on your life, and there was something that you would like to tell the people that are watching your videotape about your past experiences and what you've learned from it or how you feel, what would that be?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I learned-- I have a lot of guilt feelings. Because I feel somehow I didn't act fast enough or good enough, or I didn't find out what I could do to save them. I will always have those guilt feelings. I will always feel that I was somehow to blame. And that you should act as soon as you can, whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about a message? Is there any message?

speaker: interviewee
text: A message? What kind of a message you'd like me to leave?

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know-- if you have any thoughts.

speaker: interviewee
text: My thoughts are only is-- always, all my life, I miss my family. [CRYING] And when I actually-- [SOBBING] I wanted them to have a grandmother and a grandfather. They never had anybody. They had their father's grandparents, who were not very nice, and their whole family was never very nice to me, even though they were Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] His mother wanted us to have an orthodox wedding. I didn't want any wedding, because I had no family. I wasn't going to go through that. We went to City Hall and got married. And that was OK with him. And then we have a little, you know, dinner that somebody prepared for us. But there wasn't going to be any-- any synagogue or anything like that. And then when my first one was a boy, she wanted us to have another religious-- and I don't whether you know about that. If the firstborn is a boy, there's some sort of a religious thing that only Orthodox do. We didn't even belong to a synagogue. I wasn't going to do it. And it was-- became very nasty. They were very nasty. The whole family-- his brother, his sister. They were very nasty because I wasn't going to do what his mother said. They didn't do it, but I had to do it. It was the funniest thing. And we never were very friendly with them. I was married 25 years when I realized I didn't really have a marriage. And we sort of lived in the same house, and I was the maid and the cook and everything. And we had a big talk, and I said, I want a divorce. I don't want to live like this. If I'm married, I want to be married. If I'm not married, then I want to live alone. So we got divorced.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any last thoughts or anything before we close?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't stay in a marriage if you feel that you don't have a marriage. I didn't want to do it when my children were little. Because I felt that they should have family, you know, and a father. And I think I was mistaken in that. I should have done it. Because then maybe I could have found another man in the meantime, but, of course, I never did. Now I don't want to. [LAUGHS] I've been alone for too long, and I find it very good to be self-sufficient and to do what you want to do when you want to do it. But if you're in a-- in a-- the only message I have, if you're in a lousy marriage, don't stay in it. And I feel my background had something to do with it. I was oversensitive in certain things. And I expected somebody-- all I ever wanted was somebody to love me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he didn't, obviously. He didn't know what that was. He didn't know what I was talking about. What do you want? I mean, I bring home the money, and then-- but we had no life. We had no-- we had no marriage. We didn't even sleep together at that point. So there wasn't any reason to stay in that kind of a marriage. And I felt, what am I doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do have to look out for myself, finally. And that was the thing. Do it for yourself. I had never done-- it was always for somebody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't be afraid to do something for yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Joseph Schachter. He was born in 19-- 1891 in March. And this was-- this picture must have been taken in the 1930s in Germany, because he looks pretty good there. Oh, this is my mother. Her name was Lena Richter before she was married to my father. And as you can tell, this was done in 1939. It was already during bad times. She looks very sad. This was after my father was already in the concentration camp. And I think this picture was taken for a passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my brother Alfons. He was born January 25, 1925. And this was taken in-- when did you say it was taken? 1939? '38.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, '38. This was just before Hitler's Anschluss. And he still looks like a young boy. There's another picture of him after, where he looks totally different, if you want to take that. This is my first cousin, Kurt Richter, my mother's brother's, Artur's, only son. He's the one who committed suicide at 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture must have been taken when he was about 15 or 16, so in the-- before the Hilter Anschluss, in 1935 or '36.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this pictures taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Vienna, Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, on the right-hand side is my son, Paul Childs. He was born in 1953. And this is his wife, Ulyssa Childs. And she was born-- let me see--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This must have been taken-- she was pregnant with their first child then. So it must have been-- he was born in 1991. So he must-- must have been 1990.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Los Angeles-- Los Angeles, California. This is my younger son, Jeffrey Childs, who was-- who was born in '57. And those are my-- his two children. On the left is Jennifer, who was 10 this year, and Nicholas. So, this was taken maybe around the same time, 1991, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she's 10 now in 1995.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's 10 now, yeah. Yeah. So, she was like six and he was like four, something like that. They're two years apart.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is taken in Seattle. They live in Issaquah, and he works in Seattle. These are children of Paul and Ulyssa Childs, my oldest son and my daughter-in-law. On the right-hand side is Jordan, who is going be four next-- next January. And next to him is his little brother, Tobin, who is now 20 months old.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken probably in Agoura.

speaker: interviewee
text: Agoura, California?

speaker: interviewee
text: California.
